children.value: for Attempts 1 and 2. The only problem with Attempt 2 was its size (6cm diameter). Attempt 3 is twice this. Attempt 1 assumed square stitches, which was a mistake, as the colour work method involved keeping unused yarns inside each stitch, making the dcs taller.

children.alt: Earlier versions of this pattern. Attempt 1 (left): R=2.4, r=0.8, w=h=0.4; Attempt 2 (right)  R = 2.4, r = 0.8, w = 0.48, h=0.863.

w=0.48w = 0.48w=0.48

children.value: Attempt 2 (right) resulted in a less distorted torus than Attempt 1 (left), and with more circular cross-sections. This illustrates the difficulty of measuring stitch dimensions from only one stitch. Better choices for w and h were calculated by measuring the two characteristic circumferences of the Attempt 1 crocheted torus and dividing by number of rows/stitches, as appropriate.

children.value: Again, too many rows for the stitch dimensions. However, it is less noticeable than in
, since the stitch height is smaller.

The triangulation has 12 vertices, as this is what would be needed for the classical tonnetz (of pitch classes). Below is a diagram I used to create it. Eventually I will make a crochet pattern that directly incorporates it via stitch colouring.
Here is a nice web app for investigating  tonnetze.

children.value: Despite not matching the eventually pattern, my mistakes seem to have cancelled each other out resulting in quite a nice looking torus. At some stage I will remake this using the latest version of the pattern for these parameters, for comparison.
The knot tied onto it is a
